# Make as Cross-Language Pipeline Glue

Chapter 11 turned a single analysis step into a script that
fails loudly. A real project is not one step. Ours generates
synthetic data, cleans it, forms a portfolio, draws a figure,
runs a regression in a different language, and renders a
report. Run those by hand, in order, every time, and sooner
or later you will skip a step, run them out of order, or
rebuild the figure from last week's data and not notice. The
shell will happily let you: it runs what you type, in the
order you type it, and remembers nothing.

This chapter is about the tool that does remember. `make`
reads a description of which file is built from which, and
rebuilds exactly what is out of date, in the right order,
from one command. For a research project this is the
difference between "I think the paper matches the current
data" and "one command rebuilt the paper from the raw inputs
and it passed its own checks." Make is old, a little cranky,
and installed almost everywhere, which is why it is the glue
that holds a cross-language pipeline together. It is the
reproducibility showcase of this whole book.

A note on where the output comes from. The full
cross-language build, the incremental rebuilds, the
timestamp behavior, and `make check` are captured on the
author's Mac (macOS, GNU Make 3.81, R 4.5.3, Quarto 1.9.36;
`transcripts/ch12-make-mac.txt`), because R and Quarto do not
run in this book's Linux sandbox. The generic Make mechanics
and the Python half of the pipeline are captured in the
sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04, GNU Make 4.3; the `ch12-*` transcript
files). The Stata and EViews recipes near the end are
illustrative and never executed, and are labeled as such.

## What a build tool is for

A build tool answers one question well: given a set of files
that are built from other files, what is the least work
needed to bring everything up to date? You describe the
"built from" relationships once. After that, `make` compares
file timestamps and runs only the steps whose inputs changed.

Keep the division of labor straight, because it is the same
distinction from Chapter 2. The shell runs commands. A script
runs a fixed list of commands every time. Make decides *which*
commands need to run and in *what order*, then hands them to
the shell. It does not version your files (that is Git, the
companion book) and it does not reproduce your environment
(that is uv and renv, Chapter 13). It orchestrates. That
narrow job is exactly what a multi-step, multi-language
pipeline needs.

## The study as a dependency graph

Our running example is a chain. Each script reads one or two
files and writes the next:

```text
00_make_data.py  ->  firm_panel.csv + factors.csv
01_clean.py      ->  analysis.parquet
02_portfolio.py  ->  portfolio.{parquet,csv}
03_figure.py     ->  cumret.png
04_regression.R  ->  ff5_alpha.{tex,txt}
report.qmd       ->  report.html / report.pdf
```

Read top to bottom it is the analysis story: simulate a
firm-month panel from a seed, rebuild and lag the mispricing
signal, sort firms into a long-short portfolio, plot its
cumulative return, regress that return on the five
Fama-French factors, and assemble the table and figure into a
short report. Read as arrows, it is a dependency graph, and a
graph is exactly what `make` consumes. The figure depends on
the portfolio, which depends on the clean sample, which
depends on the raw panel, which depends on the generator
script. Change the generator and everything downstream is
stale. Change only `report.qmd` and nothing upstream needs to
move.

Notice the language boundary sitting in the middle of the
chain. Steps 00 through 03 are Python; step 04 is R; the
report is Quarto driving LaTeX. Nothing about the graph cares.
Make sees files and the commands that produce them, not
languages, which is precisely why it can glue a Python data
pipeline to an R regression to a Quarto paper without any of
them knowing about the others.

## Anatomy of a rule, and the TAB trap

A Makefile is a list of rules. Each rule has three parts: a
**target** (the file to build), its **prerequisites** (the
files it is built from), and a **recipe** (the shell commands
that build it). Here is the rule for our clean sample, with
the variables spelled out:

```makefile
data/clean/analysis.parquet: scripts/01_clean.py \
                             data/raw/firm_panel.csv
	uv run python scripts/01_clean.py
```

The target is on the left of the colon, its two prerequisites
on the right, and the recipe is the indented line below. Read
it aloud: "`analysis.parquet` is built from `01_clean.py` and
`firm_panel.csv`; to build it, run this command." If either
prerequisite is newer than the target, `make` runs the recipe.

The single most common way to break a Makefile is on that
indented line.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Recipe lines must be indented with a real TAB, never spaces.
This is the oldest trap in Make. `cat -et` makes the
indentation visible: a TAB shows as `^I`, spaces show as
spaces, and `$` marks each line end. With eight spaces
instead of a TAB, Make stops with a `missing separator`
error:

```bash
$ cat -et Makefile
h:$
        echo hi$

$ make
Makefile:2: *** missing separator (did you mean TAB instead of 8 spaces?).  Stop.
```

Indent the recipe with a TAB and it runs. If your editor
inserts spaces for tabs, turn that off for Makefiles; in VS
Code the "Makefile" language mode already does.
:::

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
Two portability notes hide in that one example. First, `cat
-et` is the BSD (macOS) spelling. GNU coreutils on Linux also
accepts `cat -A` for the same view, but macOS `cat` has no
`-A`. Second, the Make versions differ: macOS ships GNU Make
**3.81** (2006), while Linux and the sandbox here run 4.x. The
`missing separator` message is identical on both (3.81 prints
the same hint, verified on the author's Mac), so you cannot
read the version from the error. The gap is not cosmetic
anyway: it changes how Make compares timestamps, as the
next-but-one section shows. For a modern Make on a Mac, `brew
install make` installs it as `gmake`.
:::

## Automatic variables (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Writing the target and prerequisites out twice, once in the
header and again in the recipe, is repetitive and a source of
bugs when a filename changes. Make provides **automatic
variables** that stand in for them: `$@` is the target, `$<`
is the first prerequisite, and `$^` is the whole prerequisite
list. A throwaway rule makes them concrete (`a.txt` holds
`alpha`, `b.txt` holds `beta`):

```bash
$ cat Makefile
combined.txt: a.txt b.txt
	@echo 'target      $@ = $@'
	@echo 'first prereq $< = $<'
	@echo 'all prereqs  $^ = $^'
	cat $^ > $@

$ make
target      $@ = combined.txt
first prereq $< = a.txt
all prereqs  $^ = a.txt b.txt
cat a.txt b.txt > combined.txt
```

The last recipe line, `cat $^ > $@`, reads as "concatenate
all prerequisites into the target" without naming any file
twice. Rename the target and the recipe follows automatically.

These pair naturally with **pattern rules**, where a `%`
matches a stem shared by target and prerequisite. A single
rule like

```makefile
%.csv: %.raw
	clean-it $< > $@
```

builds any `.csv` from the matching `.raw`. Our pipeline uses
explicit rules instead, because each step is bespoke (a
different script, different inputs) and there is no repeated
pattern to factor out. Pattern rules earn their keep when many
files share one recipe; six unique steps do not.

## One command builds the paper

Here is the spine of our Makefile, the rule chain that turns
the dependency graph into something `make` can run. Variables
at the top name the tools and the files; the rules wire them
together:

```makefile
PY      := uv run python
RSCRIPT := Rscript
QUARTO  := quarto

.PHONY: all check clean help
all: report.html

data/raw/firm_panel.csv: scripts/00_make_data.py
	$(PY) scripts/00_make_data.py
data/raw/factors.csv: data/raw/firm_panel.csv ;

data/clean/analysis.parquet: scripts/01_clean.py \
                             data/raw/firm_panel.csv
	$(PY) scripts/01_clean.py

data/clean/portfolio.parquet: scripts/02_portfolio.py \
        data/clean/analysis.parquet data/raw/factors.csv
	$(PY) scripts/02_portfolio.py
data/clean/portfolio.csv: data/clean/portfolio.parquet ;

output/figures/cumret.png: scripts/03_figure.py \
                           data/clean/portfolio.parquet
	$(PY) scripts/03_figure.py

output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex: scripts/04_regression.R \
                             data/clean/portfolio.csv
	$(RSCRIPT) scripts/04_regression.R

report.html: report.qmd output/figures/cumret.png \
             output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex
	$(QUARTO) render report.qmd --to html
```

Two small things earn a word. Two of the steps write *two*
files in one run: the generator writes the panel and the
factors, and the portfolio step writes a `.parquet` and a
`.csv`. The bare-semicolon lines, `data/raw/factors.csv:
data/raw/firm_panel.csv ;` and `data/clean/portfolio.csv:
data/clean/portfolio.parquet ;`, are empty recipes that tell
Make "this file rides along with that one," so each generator
runs once, not once per output, and the ride-along file (the
`.csv` the R step reads) still has a rule. The `all:
report.html` line names the default goal: typing `make` with
no target builds `all`, which builds the report, which pulls
the whole graph behind it.

Now the payoff. One command, from raw scripts to rendered
paper, crossing three languages on the way:

```bash
$ make
uv run python scripts/00_make_data.py
[...]
wrote data/raw/factors.csv  months=180
wrote data/raw/firm_panel.csv  rows=54000  firms=300
seed=20260629  lambda=0.00149  sigma=0.06
content-sha256=49b3a1733cf2...
uv run python scripts/01_clean.py
wrote data/clean/analysis.parquet  rows=53700  cols=4
months=179  firms=300
uv run python scripts/02_portfolio.py
wrote data/clean/portfolio.parquet  months=179
wrote data/clean/portfolio.csv
mean ls_ret=0.004126  sd=0.016625
uv run python scripts/03_figure.py
wrote output/figures/cumret.png  final cumulative return=103.95%
Rscript scripts/04_regression.R

alpha (monthly) = 0.0034   t = 2.635
alpha %/mo = 0.3412   nobs = 179
quarto render report.qmd --to html
[...]
Output created: report.html
```

Make ran four Python steps, one R step, and one Quarto render,
in dependency order, because it walked the graph from
`report.html` back to the scripts and found everything out of
date on a clean tree.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
This is the whole argument of the book in one command. The
paper is not a file you maintain by hand alongside the data;
it is the output of a graph rooted in a seed. Anyone with the
repository runs `make` and gets the same `report.html`, the
same figure, and the same alpha, because every arrow in the
graph is executable code. The synthetic data is regenerated
from the seed rather than shipped, which is the same
discipline licensed data demands (rebuild from code, never
commit the data) and the reason the example is synthetic in
the first place.
:::

## Rebuilding only what changed

The reason to use Make instead of a script that runs every
step is that Make runs *only the stale steps*. Immediately
after a build, nothing is out of date:

```bash
$ make
make: Nothing to be done for `all'.
```

Touch one upstream file and Make rebuilds only what sits
downstream of it. Touching the portfolio script invalidates
the portfolio, and the cascade flows through the figure, the
table, and the report:

```bash
$ touch scripts/02_portfolio.py

$ make
uv run python scripts/02_portfolio.py
wrote data/clean/portfolio.parquet  months=179
wrote data/clean/portfolio.csv
mean ls_ret=0.004126  sd=0.016625
uv run python scripts/03_figure.py
wrote output/figures/cumret.png  final cumulative return=103.95%
Rscript scripts/04_regression.R

alpha (monthly) = 0.0034   t = 2.635
alpha %/mo = 0.3412   nobs = 179
quarto render report.qmd --to html
[...]
Output created: report.html
```

The data generator and the cleaning step did not run: nothing
they produce changed. Touch the regression script instead and
only the table and the report rebuild, because nothing
upstream of the regression depends on it:

```bash
$ touch scripts/04_regression.R

$ make
Rscript scripts/04_regression.R

alpha (monthly) = 0.0034   t = 2.635
alpha %/mo = 0.3412   nobs = 179
quarto render report.qmd --to html
[...]
Output created: report.html
```

To preview what a build *would* do without running anything,
`make -n` prints the recipes and stops:

```bash
$ touch scripts/04_regression.R

$ make -n
Rscript scripts/04_regression.R
quarto render report.qmd --to html
```

This selective rebuild is the feature. On a project where the
data step takes an hour, you do not want to rerun it because
you edited a figure caption.

## When timestamps lie (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Make decides "out of date" by comparing modification times: a
target is stale if any prerequisite is newer. That rule is
simple, fast, and occasionally wrong, and it is worth seeing
*why* before you trust a build blindly.

You can watch the rule decide, deterministically, by setting
the timestamps by hand. In a throwaway project where `out.txt`
is copied from `in.txt`, force both files to the same
whole-second time with `touch -t`, and Make calls the target
up to date:

```bash
$ touch -t 202606301200.00 in.txt out.txt

$ stat -f '%Fm  %N' in.txt out.txt
1782835200.000000000  in.txt
1782835200.000000000  out.txt

$ make
make: `out.txt' is up to date.
```

Equal is not "newer," so nothing rebuilds. Make `in.txt` one
second newer and the recipe runs:

```bash
$ touch -t 202606301200.01 in.txt

$ stat -f '%Fm  %N' in.txt out.txt
1782835201.000000000  in.txt
1782835200.000000000  out.txt

$ make
cp in.txt out.txt
```

That is the entire decision procedure: a newer prerequisite
rebuilds, the same age or older skips. It is clean here
because we set the times to the whole second by hand. The
failures start when the clock runs finer than Make can read.

The trouble is resolution. GNU Make 3.81, the version Apple
ships, compares timestamps at one-second granularity. The
filesystem records nanoseconds, but 3.81 throws the fraction
away. So if a prerequisite is rebuilt in the *same integer
second* as the target that depends on it, Make sees the two as
the same age, decides the target is not older, and skips it.
The same `touch scripts/04_regression.R; make` that rebuilt
the table and the report above will, on a fast machine,
sometimes rebuild the table and *not* re-render the report,
because the new table landed in the same second as the old
report. Run it twice and you can get two different answers.
The mtimes from one run that did rebuild correctly show the
margin involved:

```bash
$ stat -f '%Fm  %N' output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex report.html
1783601038.862631182  output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex
1783601041.000129705  report.html
```

Here the table and the report are 2.14 seconds apart, in
different integer seconds, so Make rebuilt correctly. Shave
that gap below a second and 3.81 cannot tell them apart.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
This is a macOS hazard specifically. GNU Make 4.x, which
Linux distributions ship (4.3 in this book's sandbox), reads
sub-second timestamps and does not have the tie problem. The
fix on a Mac is to stop relying on the system Make: `brew
install make` gives you `gmake` 4.x. The deeper lesson is that
*timestamps are a proxy for "did the content change," and the
proxy can fail*. That is the motivation for the next section.
:::

## Smoke checks that fail loudly

If timestamps can lie, do not let the build be the only judge
of whether the result is right. Our Makefile carries a `check`
target that re-derives the two numbers that define the study,
the data hash and the alpha, from the actual files, and
fails if either has drifted:

```bash
$ make check
uv run python scripts/check.py
smoke checks:
  PASS  data content hash matches (49b3a1733cf2...)
  PASS  long-short alpha = 0.0034 (t=2.64)
  PASS  figure exists (output/figures/cumret.png)
  PASS  R table alpha = 0.0034 (ff5_alpha.tex)

all checks passed
```

The check recomputes the SHA-256 of the raw returns and
re-runs the regression independently, rather than trusting the
pipeline's own arithmetic. When a result drifts, the check
says so and returns a nonzero exit code, which stops any
script or CI job built on top of it. To see the failure path,
corrupt the table by hand and re-run:

```bash
$ sed -i '' 's/0.0034/0.0099/' output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex

$ make check
uv run python scripts/check.py
smoke checks:
  PASS  data content hash matches (49b3a1733cf2...)
  PASS  long-short alpha = 0.0034 (t=2.64)
  PASS  figure exists (output/figures/cumret.png)
  FAIL  output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex: alpha 0.0099 != locked 0.0034 (stale table?)

1 check(s) FAILED
make: *** [check] Error 1
```

The check caught a table that disagreed with the data, exactly
the kind of silent drift a timestamp tie can hide.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
A failed check means a built file no longer matches its
inputs. The honest fix is to rebuild it from source, not to
hand-edit it back. `make -B output/tables/ff5_alpha.tex`
forces the table to rebuild regardless of timestamps, after
which `make check` passes again. `make -B` (build everything
unconditionally) is also the blunt escape hatch whenever you
suspect a timestamp tie has confused Make: it sidesteps the
mtime logic entirely.
:::

## .PHONY: targets that are not files (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

`all`, `check`, `clean`, and `help` name actions, not files.
There is no file called `check` on disk, so if a directory
ever did contain one, Make would see it as "already built" and
skip the recipe. Declaring them phony tells Make these targets
never correspond to files and their recipes always run:

```makefile
.PHONY: all check clean help

help:
	@echo "make        build the study (-> report.html)"
	@echo "make check  re-derive hash + alpha, check files"
	@echo "make clean  remove generated files"
```

A `help` target that lists the real entry points is a small
kindness to the next person, who is often you in six months.
The `clean` target removes generated files so you can rebuild
from nothing, which is the surest test that the graph is
complete.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`make clean` runs `rm -f` and `rm -rf` with no confirmation.
In our Makefile it deletes only generated, regenerable files
(data, figures, the report), which is safe by design. But a
`clean` recipe is just shell, and a wrong path in one, for
example `rm -rf $(BUILD)/` when `BUILD` is empty and expands
to `rm -rf /`, is the kind of irreversible mistake Chapter 7
warns about. Read a `clean` recipe before you trust it, keep
its paths inside the project, and never let a variable that
could be empty sit next to `rm -rf`.
:::

## The Python tier runs anywhere (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Because Make orchestrates files and shell commands, the parts
of the graph written in a portable language are portable too.
The Python half of our pipeline (steps 00 through 03) runs
unchanged in the locked-down Linux sandbox, with no R and no
Quarto installed, and reproduces the locked data hash and the
final cumulative return (103.95%):

```bash
$ make output/figures/cumret.png
uv run python scripts/00_make_data.py
[... uv installs the pinned environment ...]
wrote data/raw/factors.csv  months=180
wrote data/raw/firm_panel.csv  rows=54000  firms=300
seed=20260629  lambda=0.00149  sigma=0.06
content-sha256=49b3a1733cf2...
uv run python scripts/01_clean.py
[...]
uv run python scripts/02_portfolio.py
[...]
uv run python scripts/03_figure.py
wrote output/figures/cumret.png  final cumulative return=103.95%
```

Naming a specific target, `make output/figures/cumret.png`,
builds just that file and its prerequisites and stops short of
the R and Quarto steps the sandbox cannot run. The same hash
appears here as on the Mac, which is the cross-machine
determinism the seed promises. The smoke check run directly,
`uv run python scripts/check.py`, confirms the data and alpha
and reports the R table as not built here rather than failing:

```bash
$ uv run python scripts/check.py
smoke checks:
  PASS  data content hash matches (49b3a1733cf2...)
  PASS  long-short alpha = 0.0034 (t=2.64)
  PASS  figure exists (output/figures/cumret.png)
  SKIP  R table not built here (run on the Mac)

all checks passed
```

This is the toolchain split made concrete: the portable tier
runs anywhere, the R and Quarto tiers run where they are
installed, and the same Makefile drives both.

## Stata and EViews in the graph (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Not every tool a social scientist uses is scriptable from a
Unix shell in the same way. Two common ones, Stata and EViews,
slot into a Make graph differently, and our pipeline keeps
them out of the executable core on purpose so every shown
output stays real. The recipes below are **illustrative and
are not run in this book.**

Stata runs in batch with `stata -b`, which executes a do-file
and writes a log. As a Make rule it looks like any other, the
target being whatever the do-file produces:

```makefile
# illustrative; not run here
output/tables/summary.tex: code/summary.do \
                           data/clean/analysis.dta
	stata -b do code/summary.do
```

The shape is identical to the R rule: a target, its data and
script prerequisites, and a one-line batch recipe. A reader on
a machine with Stata could drop this straight into the graph.

EViews is the harder case, and the honest answer is to not
pretend it runs in the pipeline at all. EViews is effectively
Windows-only and interactive, so it does not belong on the
critical path of a portable build. Treat its output as an
**upstream manual step**: the researcher runs the EViews
program separately, exports a result (a CSV, say), and commits
that artifact. Make then consumes it as a prerequisite it
never knows how to build:

```makefile
# illustrative; not run here.
# data/external/eviews_var.csv is produced by hand in
# EViews on Windows and committed; Make only reads it.
output/figures/irf.png: scripts/plot_irf.py \
                        data/external/eviews_var.csv
	$(PY) scripts/plot_irf.py
```

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
This is the general pattern for any tool that will not run in
your build environment, not just EViews: make its output a
committed input rather than a built target. The pipeline stays
portable and fully executable for everyone, and the
non-portable step is quarantined to a documented manual
hand-off. State plainly in the README which files are produced
this way, so a replicator knows what they cannot regenerate
from code alone.
:::

## just, and when to outgrow Make (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Two questions come up once a pipeline grows. First, why not
`just`? `just` is a modern command runner with a friendlier
syntax and no TAB trap, and it is pleasant for collections of
named tasks (`just test`, `just deploy`). But it is a *task
runner*, not a *build system*: it does not track file
dependencies or skip up-to-date work. For a reproducible
analysis where "rebuild only what changed" is the entire
point, that is the feature you would be giving up. Use `just`
for convenience commands; use `make` for the dependency graph.

Second, when does Make stop being enough? When the graph grows
to dozens of steps, or you need to fan a step out over many
inputs in parallel, or you want the rebuild trigger to be
content hashes rather than timestamps, you have outgrown Make.
The usual next steps are Snakemake (Python) or `targets` (R),
both of which keep the dependency-graph idea and add
content-based change detection, richer parallelism, and better
logs. The mental model you built here carries straight over;
the syntax changes, the graph does not.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

1. Clone the example, run `make`, then run `make` again. Read
   the second run's output: why does it say there is nothing to
   do?

2. Run `make clean`, then `make output/figures/cumret.png`.
   Which steps run, and which are skipped? Explain the choice
   from the dependency graph.

3. `touch scripts/01_clean.py` and run `make -n`. Predict the
   recipes it will print before you read them, then check.

4. Break a recipe's indentation by replacing its leading TAB
   with spaces (use `cat -et` on macOS, or `cat -A` on
   GNU/Linux, to confirm), run `make`, and read the error.
   Restore the TAB.

5. Add a `.PHONY` target `figure` that builds only the figure
   (`figure: output/figures/cumret.png`), so `make figure` is a
   readable alias. Confirm `make help` is the natural place to
   document it.

6. Reproduce the timestamp rule yourself: in an empty
   directory, write a Makefile with `out.txt: in.txt` and a
   `cp in.txt out.txt` recipe, create both files, then
   `touch -t` them to the same time and run `make`. Confirm it
   skips. Make `in.txt` one second newer and confirm it
   rebuilds.
